title: Treaty Formation and Entry into Force Under International Law

content.summary: Treaties are formed through negotiation, signature, ratification, and entry into force according to procedures established by the Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties (1969).
content.body: Treaty formation follows a multi-stage process: (1) Negotiation: States or international organizations negotiate terms through diplomatic conferences or working groups. Text adoption typically requires consensus or two-thirds vote. (2) Signature: Representatives sign the treaty, often subject to ratification. Signature alone may create obligation to refrain from acts defeating treaty's object and purpose (VCLT Art. 18). (3) Ratification: Domestic approval process varies by state; US requires Senate advice and consent (two-thirds); UK requires parliamentary scrutiny; France requires constitutional review. (4) Instrument Exchange: States deposit instruments of ratification with designated depositary (UN Secretary-General for multilateral treaties). (5) Entry into Force: Treaty specifies conditions (e.g., '30 days after 50th ratification'); Paris Agreement entered into force after 55 parties representing 55% of emissions ratified. (6) Reservations: States may exclude/modify legal effect of provisions unless prohibited or incompatible with treaty's object/purpose (VCLT Art. 19-23). Customary international law binds all states; treaties bind only parties (pacta sunt servanda).
key_points: Negotiation concludes with text adoption by consensus or vote
Signature creates good faith obligation even before ratification
Ratification procedures vary by domestic constitutional law
Entry into force depends on treaty-specified thresholds
Reservations permitted unless incompatible with treaty purpose
Pacta sunt servanda: treaties binding on parties must be performed
examples: Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations (1961): Entered into force April 24, 1964 after 22 ratifications
Paris Climate Agreement (2015): Entered into force November 4, 2016; unique dual threshold of 55 parties/55% emissions
US New START Treaty (2010): Signed April 2010, Senate ratified 71-26, entered into force February 2011

sources.citation: Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties, 1155 UNTS 331 (1969)
Aust, Anthony. Modern Treaty Law and Practice. Cambridge University Press, 3rd ed., 2013.

temporal_scope.notes: VCLT codified customary law; some major powers (US, China) not parties but follow its provisions as customary law
